titleStmt.title: Letter from John Chamberlain to Dudley Carleton, 20 May 1598

publicationStmt.p: Published digitally at earlymodernletters.org. Licensed under CC BY 4.0.

div.p: I wrote to you the 17th of this present, since which time I receved yours of the 24th of Aprill, which had playde the truand I know not where, and loitered very longe after his fellowes. Yet yt brought goode contentment with yt, to see your state somwhat amended, and your fortune rising, which yf you once lay hold on I make no doubt but you will manage to your best advantage. Your brother Carleton comes not to towne this terme. I had a letter from him on Wensday last to provide two bookes you write for, wherin I have don my best indevor, and with much ado and longe search found the one, but the other will not be heard of, yet casting about I remembred myself that of mine owne store I had a treatise of the siege of Paris written in Spanish by a Spaniard that was all the while in the towne, which yf yt may serve your turne is at your disposing; otherwise, yf you have no great use of yt (because Spanish bookes are hard to come by) I could be content you send or bring yt back when you shall thincke best.

BarneveltJohan van Oldenbarnevelt, agent of the Netherlands, who with other commissioners and Noel Caron, the agent resident in England, conferred with commissioners appointed by the Queen. (Camden, Annales, p. 496.) the agent and advocate of the States is here and hath had audience these two dayes together, but I feare we are deafe on that side, and no musike will please us unles yt be to the tune of peace. One of the cheifest reasons I can heare for yt, is a kind of disdaine and envie at our neighboures welldoinge: in that we for theyre sake and defence entring into the warre, and being barred from all commerce and entercourse of marchandise, they in the meane time thrust us out of all trafficke to our utter undoing (yf in time yt be not looked into), and theyre owne advancement: and though the feare of the Spaniards recovering those countries and increasing greatnes do somwhat trouble us, yet yt is thought but a weake pollicie for feare of future and uncertain daunger (which many accidents may divert) to indure a present and certaine losse. And come what can come, Fraunce and England holding together (as in all probabilitie and reason they are like) they shall alwayes be able to make theyre partie goode with Spaine. An other motive to the peace is the troubles of Ireland, which are like to put the Quene to exceding charge, and withall there appeares a blacke clowde in Scotland that threatens a storme.The alleged conspiracy revealed by Valentine Thomas. Cf. Letter 2.

These amonge many other reasons that I have heard and sene are in mine opinion most materiall: together with an offer from the Spaniard (as they say) that come peace or war with the States, the Quene shall hold the cautionarie townes fowre yeares, in which space yf the States do not repay the mony she hath disbursed for them, he upon those townes will see her satisfied, which semes a matter of such importaunce to some men that with such a masse of mony they thincke we shalbe able to defend our selves against the world.

Other newes here goes very lowe, only Sir William HarvyWilliam Hervey (created Lord Hervey of Kidbrooke, 1628) in May, 1598, married Mary, widow of Henry Wriothesley, second Earl of Southampton, and of Sir Thomas Heneage. Cf. Letter 6. is sayde to have married the Countesse of Southampton. Master Dormer and his wife came to towne yesternight and lodge at your brother Williams for some fowre or five dayes.

closer.salute: Thus with my goode wishes for your health and welfare I commit you to God.

address.addrLine: To my assured goode frend Master Dudley Carleton attending on the Lord Governor of Ostend.

div.head: Editor's Note
div.p: Written from London on 20 May 1598, just three days after Letter 3, this brief letter to Dudley Carleton in Ostend turns on the question of Anglo-Spanish peace and England's relationship with the Dutch States-General. Chamberlain opens with domestic business: a delayed letter from Carleton, an unsuccessful search for two books requested by Carleton's brother George, and the offer of a Spanish treatise on the siege of Paris from his own library. The letter's main body is a sustained analysis of the case for peace: English resentment at Dutch commercial exclusion, the deepening crisis in Ireland, and an unnamed conspiracy in Scotland. Chamberlain also relays the Spanish offer to guarantee England's possession of the cautionary towns — Flushing and Brill — for four years as security against unpaid loans to the Dutch. The letter closes with two brief social notes: Sir William Harvey's reported marriage to Mary, the widowed Countess of Southampton, and the arrival of Michael Dormer and his wife in London, lodging with Alexander Williams.
